𝗜𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗼𝗻𝗹𝘆 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲 𝗜 𝗵𝗲𝗮𝗿 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗶𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱 𝘀𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗺𝗲, 𝗜'𝗺 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝗽𝗼𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗼 𝘆𝗼𝘂. 
And if you just got laid off, that's the most important sentence you'll read this week, because that's exactly the trap you're about to walk into.

Look, I get it. You just got laid off. You're scared. Your brain is screaming, Reach out to everyone 𝘕𝘖𝘞. And that instinct makes sense. 

But here's what happens when you act on that panic: you open LinkedIn, scroll past names you haven't talked to in two or three years, and fire off messages that say, 'Heyyy long time! Quick q, know anyone hiring?'

I'm not saying you're a user. But I am telling you, that's exactly how it lands. Because without a real relationship underneath it, that message reads as pure extraction. 𝗔𝗻𝗱 𝗻𝗼𝗯𝗼𝗱𝘆 𝘄𝗮𝗻𝘁𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗳𝗲𝗲𝗹 𝗹𝗶𝗸𝗲 𝗮 𝘃𝗲𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗺𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗲.

Here's what you do instead.

𝗙𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁, 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘁 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗽𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲. 
The ones you actually know, like, and trust. The former coworker you'd actually grab lunch with. The friend from your last job who you'd invite to a barbecue. Reach out to them first, not with a big ask, but with honesty. Say, 'Hey, I just got laid off. I'm not asking for a favor yet, but I'd love your advice on who I should talk to, and if you'd be willing to introduce me.' 

𝗦𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗱, 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗽𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗵𝗮𝘃𝗲𝗻'𝘁 𝘁𝗮𝗹𝗸𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗼 𝗶𝗻 𝗮 𝘄𝗵𝗶𝗹𝗲, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝘄𝗲𝗿𝗲𝗻'𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗰𝗹𝗼𝘀𝗲 𝘁𝗼 𝗯𝗲𝗴𝗶𝗻 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵, 𝗱𝗼 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗱 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗮𝗻 𝗮𝘀𝗸. 
You lead with a rebuild. Send a message that says, 'Hey, I know it's been a minute. Coffee or lunch on me? No ask, just genuinely want to catch up.' Then show up. Be curious about their life. Rebuild the relationship before you even think about needing something from it. And if after that catch-up the moment feels right? You can say, 'By the way, I'm actually looking, if you hear of anything, keep me in mind.' That's respectful. That's human. That's not opportunistic, that's just good sense.

𝗧𝗵𝗶𝗿𝗱, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗜'𝗺 𝗴𝗼𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗼 𝗯𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘆𝗼𝘂.
Start tending to yo

STAR is great but you end up getting stuck in the situation for too long and lose people's attention.

To learn more about how to use the HERO method comment 'YouTube' and I'll send you a link to watch more.

Throwing it back to 9 years ago, when I was invited by @persianwomenintech to speak at Zillow HQ in San Francisco along with other amazing women on the future of hiring. 

The caliber of talent in the audience was staggering. I met so many women with their PhD's in STEM and so many of them were so creative. They wanted to find purpose in their work and figuring out ways to balance their talent with their profession.

Its always wonderful to find moments where you can show up and share ideas to help your community.

Think the STAR method is the best interview formula? Think again. 

After years of creating content I realized that I could turn that knowledge into a new formula that has helped people get real offers from their interviews.

Want to learn the HERO method? Comment 'YouTube' for a link to my YouTube page

A decade in recruiting.

That's the math when I sit with it. Nearly 10 years of reading resumes, running interviews, building hiring infrastructure & teams, and watching people navigate the hardest pivots of their careers.

Conversations with people who were certain they wouldn't get the job, and did. And with people who were certain they would, and didn't.

A few things a decade has taught me:

→ The best candidates are rarely the loudest. They're the clearest.

→ Most "no's" aren't about you. They're about fit, timing, headcount, internal politics, almost everything except your worth.

→ The story you tell about yourself becomes the ceiling you build for yourself. Sharpen it.

→ Recruiters aren't gatekeepers. We're translators between what you've done and what a company needs.

→ Helping someone get hired is one of the most underrated ways to change a life.

Same work, deeper intention. The next chapter is just getting started.

🌟

If you're not being found by recruiters, you don't have a networking problem you have a visibility problem. 

Recruiters and Sourcers actively source candidates for roles even before jobs are posted. Why? Because time is money and Talent Acquisition teams like to build a pipeline of candidates for roles to present to hiring teams to ensure that 1) they're on the right track and 2) get a jump start on hiring in order to decrease time to hire.

For this and more information on how hiring works, check out my latest YouTube video. Comment YOUTUBE for the link
